content:
# Mark Chapter 9 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 36v–37v (PDF pages 72–74) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_009_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_009_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_009_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ And he told them, "I tell you the truth: some of you standing here will not die before you see the kingdom of God arrive in power."

² Six days later, Jesus took Peter, James, and John and led them up a high mountain where they were completely alone. There, right before their eyes, he was transformed.

³ His clothes became blindingly white—whiter than any bleach on earth could make them.

⁴ Then Elijah and Moses appeared, and the two of them were deep in conversation with Jesus.

⁵ Peter blurted out, "Rabbi, this is incredible! Let us put up three shelters—one for you, one for Moses, and one for Elijah."

⁶ He had no idea what he was saying. They were terrified.

⁷ Then a cloud rolled in and covered them, and a voice came from the cloud: "This is my Son, whom I love. Listen to him!"

⁸ Suddenly, when they looked around, they saw no one with them anymore—only Jesus.

⁹ As they were coming down the mountain, Jesus told them not to tell anyone what they had seen until the Son of Man had risen from the dead.

¹⁰ They kept it to themselves, but kept puzzling over what "rising from the dead" could mean.

¹¹ They asked him, "Why do the religious scholars say that Elijah has to come first?"

¹² He replied, "Elijah does come first to set everything right. But then why does Scripture say that the Son of Man must suffer terribly and be rejected?

¹³ I'm telling you, Elijah has already come—and they did whatever they wanted to him, just as Scripture said they would."

¹⁴ When they got back to the other disciples, they found a large crowd surrounding them and some scribes locked in an argument with them.

¹⁵ The moment the crowd spotted Jesus, they were overcome with awe and ran to meet him.

¹⁶ "What are you arguing about?" he asked.

¹⁷ A man in the crowd spoke up: "Teacher, I brought you my son. He's possessed by a spirit that has taken away his ability to speak.

¹⁸ Whenever it takes hold of him, it slams him to the ground. He foams at the mouth, grinds his teeth, and goes stiff. I asked your disciples to drive it out, but they couldn't."

¹⁹ Jesus responded, "You faithless generation! How long must I be among you? How long must I put up with you? Bring the boy to me."

²⁰ They brought him. The moment the spirit saw Jesus, it threw the boy into violent convulsions. He fell to the ground, rolling and foaming.

²¹ "How long has he been like this?" Jesus asked the father. "Since he was little," the man replied.

²² "It has thrown him into fire and water over and over, trying to kill him. But if you can do anything—please, have mercy on us and help us!"

²³ Jesus said, "'If you can'? Everything is possible for the one who believes."

²⁴ The father instantly cried out, "I do believe—help me overcome my doubt!"

²⁵ When Jesus saw the crowd closing in, he commanded the evil spirit, "You deaf and mute spirit, I order you: come out of him and never enter him again!"

²⁶ The spirit screamed, threw the boy into terrible convulsions, and came out. The boy lay so still he looked dead. Most of the crowd said, "He's gone."

²⁷ But Jesus took his hand and pulled him to his feet, and he stood up.

²⁸ Later, when Jesus had gone inside, his disciples asked him privately, "Why couldn't we drive it out?"

²⁹ He told them, "This kind can only come out through prayer."

³⁰ They left that area and traveled through Galilee. Jesus didn't want anyone to know where they were,

³¹ because he was teaching his disciples. He told them, "The Son of Man is going to be handed over to people who will kill him. But three days after being killed, he will rise."

³² They didn't understand what he meant, and they were too afraid to ask.

³³ They arrived in Capernaum. Once they were in the house, he asked them, "What were you arguing about on the road?"

³⁴ Dead silence. On the road, they had been arguing about which of them was the greatest.

³⁵ Jesus sat down, called the twelve over, and said, "Whoever wants to be first must be the very last—and the servant of everyone."

³⁶ He took a small child, stood the child in the middle of them, wrapped his arms around the child, and said,

³⁷ "Whoever welcomes a child like this in my name welcomes me. And whoever welcomes me isn't just welcoming me—they're welcoming the one who sent me."

³⁸ John said, "Teacher, we saw someone driving out demons using your name, and we tried to stop him because he's not one of us."

³⁹ "Don't stop him," Jesus said. "No one who performs a miracle in my name will be able to turn around and bad-mouth me the next moment.

⁴⁰ Whoever is not against us is on our side.

⁴¹ Anyone who gives you even a cup of water because you belong to the Messiah—I promise you, that person will not go unrewarded.

⁴² But if anyone leads one of these trusting little ones into sin, it would be better for that person to have a massive millstone tied around their neck and be thrown into the sea.

⁴³ If your hand pulls you toward sin, cut it off. Better to enter eternal life with one hand than to keep both hands and end up in Gehenna—the fire that never goes out.

⁴⁴ [Where the worm never dies and the fire never goes out.]

⁴⁵ If your foot pulls you toward sin, cut it off. Better to enter eternal life limping than to keep both feet and be thrown into Gehenna.

⁴⁶ [Where the worm never dies and the fire never goes out.]

⁴⁷ If your eye pulls you toward sin, rip it out. Better to enter God's kingdom with one eye than to keep both eyes and be thrown into Gehenna,

⁴⁸ where the worm never dies and the fire never goes out.

⁴⁹ Everyone will be salted with fire.

⁵⁰ Salt is a good thing. But if salt loses its flavor, how can you restore it? Have salt among yourselves, and live at peace with each other."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **"I believe; help my unbelief!" (v. 24):** One of the most honest prayers in the Bible. The father doesn't pretend to have perfect faith. He admits his faith and his doubt in the same breath. This is Mark at his most psychologically real.

2. **Peter's babbling at the Transfiguration (vv. 5-6):** Mark explicitly tells us Peter "didn't know what to say" — and then proceeds to record exactly what he said. The editorial aside is vintage Mark: don't take Peter's suggestion seriously; he was speaking out of sheer terror.

3. **"Elijah has already come" (v. 13):** Jesus identifies John the Baptist as the returning Elijah prophesied in Malachi 4:5-6. The "they did to him whatever they wanted" refers to John's execution by Herod. The implication: if Elijah was treated that way, what does that mean for the Son of Man?

4. **The disciples' argument about greatness (vv. 33-34):** The irony is devastating. Jesus has just predicted his death for the second time, and they're debating who's the most important. Their silence when confronted tells us they knew it was shameful.

5. **"Salted with fire" (v. 49):** This is one of the most debated sayings in the Gospels. The connection between salt (purification/preservation) and fire (judgment/testing) remains elusive. I translate it straightforwardly and let the mystery stand.

6. **vv. 44, 46 — Absent from earliest manuscripts:** These verses repeat v. 48 and are not in Sinaiticus or Vaticanus. They were added by later scribes for symmetry.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
